abstract: Improving the efficiency of catalytic dinitrogen ($N_2$) reduction is a central challenge in developing sustainable alternatives to the Haber–Bosch process. This study explores the production of ammonia ($NH_3$) under ambient conditions utilizing molybdenum complexes supported by N-heterocyclic carbene-based PCP-type pincer ligands, with samarium diiodide ($SmI_2$) and water serving as the reductant and proton source, respectively. Kinetic analyses reveal a concentration-dependent shift in the rate-determining step: at high catalyst concentrations, the reaction rate is governed by proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) during N–H bond formation at the molybdenum imide intermediate, while at low concentrations, catalyst dimerization becomes rate-limiting. Toward optimizing this system, density functional theory calculations suggested that introducing electron-withdrawing groups onto the benzimidazole backbone of the pincer ligand would stabilize the LUMO of key nitride intermediates and strengthen the Mo–C(carbene) interaction. Guided by these insights, a series of substituted complexes were synthesized and evaluated. A trifluoromethyl-substituted molybdenum trichloride complex emerged as a exceptionally robust catalyst, achieving a turnover number of up to 60,000 equivalents of ammonia per molybdenum atom and a turnover frequency as high as 800 equivalents per minute. These results represent a significant advancement in molecular nitrogen fixation, offering a framework for the rational design of high-performance catalysts for next-generation ammonia synthesis.
